name: derris elliptica
mechanism: Rotenone inhibits NADH dehydrogenase in the electron transport chain, disrupting ATP production and causing cellular hypoxia.
contraindications: unsafe for human consumption. do not ingest or inhale powdered forms.
safetyNotes: muscle paralysis. long-term rotenone exposure linked to parkinson’s-like symptoms.
Very high. Rotenone has been used in rodenticides and insecticides.

sources.note: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1241859/;WHO Pesticide Evaluation Report – Rotenone;Traditional Plant Lore of Southeast Asia – 2010

description: a tropical vine traditionally used as a natural fish poison. contains rotenone, a potent neurotoxin that disrupts mitochondrial respiration. used in agriculture but dangerous to humans in concentrated doses.
duration: No direct duration data. Contextual inference: a tropical vine traditionally used as a natural fish poison. contains rotenone, a potent neurotoxin that disrupts mitochondrial respiration. used in agriculture but dangerous to humans in concentrated doses.. Rotenone inhibits NADH dehydrogenase in the electron transport chain, disrupting ATP production and causing cellular hypoxia.. Paralysis (insects/fish);Mild euphoria (folklore);Toxicity. southeast asia, pacific islands. ; nan; nan
legalStatus: Restricted or banned in many regions
preparation: Crushed root used to stun fish in streams (traditional method). Modern rotenone extracts banned in some countries.
therapeuticUses: no modern internal medical uses. occasionally used externally in veterinary or agricultural settings.
sideEffects: term rotenone exposure linked to parkinson’s
